narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated an 80% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which directly led to the rejection of your admission because the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile: your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA. Most influential was your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5, which strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar failure compared to higher-performing peers. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also lower than the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern by showing early academic struggles that often persist. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, dipping under the dataset average of 3.21, pushed the prediction further toward failure, as it indicated overall academic performance below what the model associates with bar success. While your LSAT score of 36 is close to the average of 36.7 and your middle family income bracket aligns with many applicants, along with your full-time student status, these did not outweigh the top concerns. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or upper deciles and maintain GPAs above 3.2, your metrics placed you in a riskier group historically linked to bar challenges. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted effort—but the model weighs these patterns heavily to guide admissions. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles and GPA, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted tutoring, and aim to retake the LSAT if feasible. Building a stronger academic narrative could shift future predictions in your favor, opening doors at other schools. You're clearly determined to pursue law; focusing on these areas now can help prove the model wrong.
